course_description:
Applied veterinary aspects of infectious disease (microbiology), including the pharmacology of anti-microbial & anti-parasitic drugs. Integration of all infectious diseases into a clinical setting. Students must enrol in VETS3010 for both semesters.

The course focuses on applied veterinary aspects of infectious disease in domestic animals. It aims to provide students with the theoretical and practical skills necessary to investigate situations where infectious agents may contribute to decreased production, illness, or mortality in animals. Students should also be able to advise future clients on zoonotic risks associated with infections in their pets or livestock, and offer strategies to minimise these risks.

The course includes modules on virology, parasitology, bacteriology and mycology. The pharmacology of antimicrobial and antiparasitic drugs is embedded within the relevant modules on parasites and bacteria.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

prerequisites: You'll need to complete the following courses before enrolling in this one:
restrictions: Enrolment restricted to Bachelor of Veterinary Science (Honours) students only.

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
Additional timetable information
Please check your course Blackboard site frequently for the latest Timetable information.
Please note that unexpected changes to the Timetable may occur throughout the Semester. Therefore, it is crucial to regularly check the course Blackboard site. Any updates to the timetable will be communicated to students via Blackboard Announcements.
course_aims: This course develops the knowledge, skills, and clinical reasoning required to investigate, diagnose, and manage infectious diseases of domestic animals. Students integrate microbiology, parasitology, and virology to assess disease risks, interpret diagnostic findings, and formulate evidence‑based treatment and control plans, including the appropriate use of antimicrobials and parasiticides. The course also prepares students to provide informed advice on zoonoses, biosecurity, and infection control within veterinary practice.

learning_outcomes.description: Identify infectious agents of veterinary importance and describe the pathogenesis and epidemiology of infectious diseases in individual animals and groups of animals.
Design and justify differential diagnoses and diagnostic strategies for infectious diseases, including the selection, performance, interpretation, and limitations of diagnostic tests.
Develop and justify immediate and longer‑term treatment, prevention, and control plans for infectious diseases, including appropriate antimicrobial and parasiticide use and stewardship principles.
Identify and assess zoonotic and public‑health risks and provide evidence‑based advice to owners, managers, and stakeholders using One Health and biosecurity principles.
Explain and apply biosecurity, biosafety, infection‑control principles, and the fundamentals of emergency response to animal diseases, including recognition of notifiable and reportable conditions.
Demonstrate core vocational laboratory and diagnostic skills, including sample collection, test performance, data interpretation, and accurate reporting.
Communicate diagnostic reasoning and infectious‑disease information clearly and accurately in written assessments and during supervised practical activities.
Integrate theoretical knowledge and practical skills from the program thus far to investigate and reason through infectious‑disease cases as preparation for clinical‑year decision‑making.

assessment_details.title: Parasitology Exam
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Students will be examined on all parasitology and parasiticide lectures, practicals, and tutorials. In addition to content‑focused questions, the exam will include case‑based questions that require students to apply parasitology knowledge to clinical or investigative scenarios, including differential diagnoses, selection and interpretation of diagnostic tests, and basic treatment or control planning. This will be an on-campus invigilated examination hosted on Inspera. The exam duration is 100 mins including 10 mins planning time. Exam questions may also include: multiple response, text entry, matching/pairing, inline choice, drag and drop, composite, hotspot and matching and case‑based applied reasoning items. Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.exam_details: Planning time 10 minutes Duration 90 minutes Calculator options (In person) Casio FX82 series only or UQ approved and labelled calculator Open/closed book Closed book examination - no written materials permitted Exam platform Inspera Invigilation Invigilated in person
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Assessment using the Inspera platform
Students must click the ‘SUBMIT NOW’ button on the summary page to submit their answers. After submission, a confirmation will appear on the dashboard indicating that the answers have been submitted and can no longer be edited.
For all exams lasting 60, 90, 120, or 180 minutes, an additional 10 minutes of planning time is provided to read the questions and plan responses, though students may start working immediately if they choose.
It’s crucial to ensure your laptop meets the device requirements for Inspera assessments before the exam. If you don’t have a suitable laptop, you can borrow one from the Library. Check Get Familiar with Inspera for the most up-to-date information about using the platform. Note that power outlets will not be available during exams.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to defer this exam .
Students must be available for their examinations on the scheduled date, time, and campus of their course enrolment. The exam format is determined by the Course Coordinator and may include written (paper-based, online, or digital), practical, oral, or other timed assessments (such as quizzes).
If you are unable to sit for your original examination, you may be eligible for a deferred examination. To apply, you must demonstrate that unavoidable circumstances prevented you from attending your exam. If this is not possible, you can apply for a one-off discretionary deferred exam. All requests for deferred examinations MUST include supporting documentation. For more information, including acceptable evidence and application instructions, please visit my.UQ Deferring an Exam .
Please note that there are no provisions to defer an already-deferred exam. You must be available to sit your deferred examination at the allocated time.
Students who submit five or more deferral requests within a twelve-month period will be contacted with advice on the services and support available within the University.

assessment_details.title: Parasitology Practical Sequence
assessment_details.due_date: (Faecal Diagnostics I) 24/03/2026 (Skin Diagnostics) 31/03/2026 (Faecal Diagnostics II) 14/04/2026 (Blood Diagnostics) 21/04/2026 (Other Diagnostics) 28/04/2026
assessment_details.task_description: Students will complete five parasitology practical classes , each focusing on key parasitology diagnostic skills. At each practical, there will be a short in‑class assessment task worth 1.25%. To allow flexibility, the best 4 out of 5 marks will count toward your final grade (total 5% ). All work submitted must be the student’s own. Collusion during practical quizzes constitutes academic misconduct. Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: Virology and Parasitology Integration Exam
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: This examination will assess two components: Part A – Virology (60 mins) (15%) This section examines content from virology lectures and tutorials. It will assess your understanding of viral pathogens, their associated diseases, diagnostic principles, and approaches to management. Part B – Integrated Case‑Based Assessment (30 mins) (5%) This section requires you to apply knowledge from both virology and parasitology in realistic clinical scenarios. You will work through cases that involve identifying likely infectious agents, developing differential diagnoses, selecting and interpreting diagnostic tests, and proposing appropriate treatment and control plans. This will be an on-campus invigilated examination hosted on Inspera. The exam duration is 100 mins including 10 mins planning time. Exam questions may also include: multiple response, text entry, matching/pairing, inline choice, drag and drop, composite, hotspot and matching, and case‑based applied‑reasoning items. Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. assessment_details.due_date: Second End of Semester Exam Period 7/11/2026 - 21/11/2026
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Students will be examined on all bacteriology and mycology lectures, tutorials, practical classes, and antimicrobial chemotherapy content. The exam will also assess material from the Public Health, Zoonoses, and Integration lectures, tutorials, as well as the Biosecurity Workshop. In addition to content‑focused questions, the exam may include items that require students to apply microbiology and public health knowledge to clinical, epidemiological, and biosecurity scenarios. These may involve developing differential diagnoses, interpreting diagnostic results, evaluating antimicrobial selection, and applying principles of population health, zoonoses, and biosecurity. This will be an on-campus invigilated examination hosted on Inspera. The exam duration is 100 mins including 10 mins planning time. Exam questions may also include: multiple response, text entry, matching/pairing, inline choice, drag and drop, composite, hotspot and matching, as well as applied reasoning items. assessment_details.title: Integration Practical Examination
assessment_details.due_date: Second End of Semester Exam Period 7/11/2026 - 21/11/2026
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: This open‑book practical examination assesses students’ clinical reasoning and their ability to integrate knowledge across bacteriology, virology, mycology, and parasitology . The exam evaluates both theoretical understanding and practical competence in diagnosing and managing infectious diseases. Students will complete two case studies , each comprising written and/or practical components. Part A - Written component (5%) - 30 minutes Based on the case scenario provided, students must: Compose a differential diagnosis list including FIVE (5) infectious agents only. Describe the samples that should be collected to make a diagnosis and explain: how the samples should be managed in‑clinic/on‑farm (correct handling procedures), and what tests the diagnostic laboratory should perform. (One case only) Complete the Veterinary Laboratory Services (VLS) sample request form, including all relevant information and required tests. Part B- Laboratory Practical Skills (10%) - 60 minutes Students will perform a set of diagnostic skills relevant to their case scenario across bacteriology, virology, mycology, and parasitology. They will also be assessed on OHS, laboratory practice, accuracy of technique, and correct recording/reporting of findings. Hurdle: Must achieve ≥50% in Part B to pass the course Part C - Written Component (5%) - 30 minutes Students will be provided with a diagnostic report for each case. For both cases, students must: Provide the final diagnosis. Provide the prognosis. Describe the final treatment and management plan based on the report findings. Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submission (Part A):
Typed responses must be submitted through the Turnitin submission point in Blackboard. Students must ensure they receive a Turnitin receipt , which is the only accepted evidence of submission. The completed VLS form must be handed to the invigilator before leaving the room.
Submission (Part B):
Written answer sheets must be submitted to the invigilators before leaving the laboratory.
Submission (Part C):
Typed responses must be submitted through the Turnitin submission point in Blackboard. Students must ensure they receive a Turnitin receipt , which is the only accepted evidence of submission.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to defer this exam .
Students must be available for their examinations on the scheduled date, time, and campus of their course enrolment. The exam format is determined by the Course Coordinator and may include written (paper-based, online, or digital), practical, oral, or other timed assessments (such as quizzes).
If you are unable to sit for your original examination, you may be eligible for a deferred examination. To apply, you must demonstrate that unavoidable circumstances prevented you from attending your exam. If this is not possible, you can apply for a one-off discretionary deferred exam. All requests for deferred examinations MUST include supporting documentation. For more information, including acceptable evidence and application instructions, please visit my.UQ Deferring an Exam .
Please note that there are no provisions to defer an already-deferred exam. You must be available to sit your deferred examination at the allocated time.
Students who submit five or more deferral requests within a twelve-month period will be contacted with advice on the services and support available within the University.

learning_activities.topic: Bacteriology and Mycology Practical Sub-activity: Prac A - Opportunistic Infections Prac B - Enteric Disease Prac C - Bovine mastitis & Gram positive cocci Prac D - Septicaemia, bacteraemia, toxaemia and chronic infection-Gram positive bacilli Prac E - Mycology Integration Practical Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07, L08

Parasitology Lectures In this module, you will cover key parasites of veterinary importance and their associated diseases, parasiticides and vocational skills Sub-activity: Para 1: Parasiticide therapeutics 1 Para 2: Parasiticide therapeutics 2 Para 3: Parasiticide therapeutics 3 Para 4: Parasiticide therapeutics 4 Para 5: Parasites of small animal gut 1 Para 6: Parasites of small animal gut 2 Para 7: Parasites of small animal gut 3 Para 8: Parasites of small animal gut 4 Para 9: Parasites of small animal skin 1 Para 10: Parasites of small animal skin 2 Para 11: Parasites of small animal skin 3 Para 12: Parasites of small animal skin 4 & tick paralysis Para 13: Cardiorespiratory parasites of small animals Para 14: Parasites of other small animal body systems Para 15: Ruminant – GI Nematodes 1 Para 16 Ruminant – GI Nematodes 2 Para 17: Ruminants GI Nematodes 3 Para 18: Ruminants Flukes and Cestodes Para19: Ruminant flystrike Para 20: Ruminant – other flies Para 21 Ruminant – lice, minor arthropods Para 22: Ruminants – ticks and tick fever Para 23: Ruminants – ticks Para 24: Equine GIT 1 Para 25: Equine GIT 2 Para 26: Equine GIT 3 Para 27: Equine – skin and eye Para 28: Ruminants – Protozoa, deer, camels Para 29: Poultry & companion birds Para 30: Poultry, companion birds and aquarium fish Para 31: Pigs Para 32: Pigs, & food-borne parasites Para 33: Food-borne parasites Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07, L08

Parasitology Tutorials In this module, you will cover key parasites of veterinary importance and their associated diseases, parasiticides and vocational skills Sub-activity: Navigating parasiticides Small animal parasites Tutorial small animal skin and ticks Tutorial - small animal ectoparasites and cardiorespiratory pathogens Ruminant GI parasites Tutorial: Rumiants ectoparasites Tutorial - equine parasites Tutorial - Pigs and food borne parasites Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07, L08

Parasitology Practical In this module, you will cover key parasites of veterinary importance and their associated diseases, parasiticides and vocational skills Sub-activity: Pre-practical tutorials:Systematic parasite identification and microscopy Laboratory 1 - Faecal diagnostics Laboratory 2: Faecal diagnostics II Laboratory 3: Skin diagnostics Laboratory 4: Blood diagnostics Laboratory 5 - Other parasite diagnostics Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07, L08

Virology Lectures In this module, you will cover key viruses of veterinary importance and their associated diseases Sub-activity: Introduction to Viruses Virology 1: Poxviruses Virology 2: Pox (cont.) & Viral Diagnostics Virology 3: Adeno, Papilloma, Parvoviruses Virology 4: Parvoviruses (cont), Circoviruses Virology 5: Herpesviruses Virology 6: Herpesviruses 2 Virology 6: Retroviruses 1 Virology 7: Retroviruses 2 Virology 8: Retroviruses 3 Virology 9: Toga and Flaviviruses 1 Virology 10: Toga and Flaviviruses 2 Virology 11: Influenza viruses Virology 12: Paramyxoviruses 1 Virology 13: Paramyxoviruses 2 Virology 14: Rhabdoviruses Virology 15: Corona & Caliciviruses Virology 16: Picornaviruses Virology 17: Reoviruses Virology 18: Birna, Bunya & Arteriviruses Virology 19: Prions Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L07, L08

Virology Tutorial In this module, you will cover key viruses of veterinary importance and their associated diseases Sub-activity: Virology tutorial Virology Tutorial 1 - case based Virology Tutorial 2 - case based Virology tutorial Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L07, L08

Bacteriology and Mycology Lectures In this module, you will cover key bacteria and fungi of veterinary importance and their associated diseases, antimicrobials and vocational skills Sub-activity: Antibacterials 1 Antibacterials 2 Antibacterials 3 Antibacterials 4 Pseudomonads Curved Rods Enterobacteriaceae I: Opportunists Enterobacteriaceae II: E. coli Enterobacteriaceae III: Salmonella Enterobacteriaceae IV: Salmonella/Yersinia Antimicrobial Resistance 1 Antimicrobial Resistance 2 Haemophilus/Actinobacillus/Bordetella/Moraxella/Taylorella Brucella Pasteurella/Mannheimia Gram-negative anaerobes Staphylococcus Streptococcus Listeria/ corynebacterium Erysipelothrix/ Rhodococcus Bacillus Clostridium Rickettsias, Chlamydias, Mycoplasma I Rickettsias, Chlamydias, Mycoplasma II Mycology I Mycology II Actinomycetes/ Mycobacterium Spirochetes Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07, L08

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . You'll also need to be aware of the following policies and procedures while completing this course: Fitness to Practise Policy Learning activities
